text: Hello, everyone. This is a video of a straightforward robotic tap right inguinal hernia repair. This is Roxanne Liu in Oakland, California. This video is sped up two times. The reason I'm showing this video is just because the patient is pretty thin, so the tissue is pretty delicate, demonstrating the precision of robotic surgery. Also, I'm doing something different at the end of the case now, so I just wanted to show that.

The surgeon begins the flap laterally with a vertical oblique incision, staying outside the retro-rectus space. As the dissection progresses medially, a horizontal incision is made across the peritoneum. In the lateral space of Bacros, the surgeon stays close to the peritoneum due to minimal preperitoneal fat, making a wide incision to gradually deepen the pocket while avoiding potential bleeding and damage to unseen structures.

The surgeon identifies the transversalis fascia of the direct inguinal hernia and uses cautery to divide small blood vessels, preventing bleeding that could obscure tissue planes. The testicular vessels are carefully separated from the peritoneum, revealing a clear areolar plane. The indirect inguinal hernia is reduced, and attention shifts to the direct inguinal hernia, with the surgeon staying close to the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon uses robotic precision to dissect along the proper plane without perforating the thin peritoneal sacs. Laterally, the surgeon pulls to expose the plane between the peritoneum and the vas deferens. Near the obturator lymph node packet, the surgeon carefully stays against the peritoneum to avoid injury. The direct inguinal hernia is reduced, and the surgeon progresses past the midline into the retro pubic space, maintaining a broad working plane.

The surgeon adjusts retraction to work across a wide area, ensuring a smooth transition for the mesh from the retropubic space to the apogryl space. Dissection is carried down to the obturator space, leaving the obturator fat intact. The surgeon prioritizes obtaining the critical view of the myopectineal orifice, exposing the iliopubic tract and identifying the general femoral nerves. The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve is less visible. The dissection extends past the midline, two centimeters deep into the retro pubic space.

The surgeon identifies a small femoral gap and positions a large 3D Max mid mesh within the pelvic region of a petite patient. The right-hand port is adjusted by the assistant, and the surgeon aligns the vertical line along the vas deferens, preparing to place a stitch at Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon ensures there is no peritoneum tucked behind the mesh and observes a robust Cooper's ligament. The medial lower edge of the flap is checked to prevent rising when the bladder distends. The mesh is positioned over the pubis and superior pubic rami, with an additional stitch placed using a 3-0 Vicryl suture to prevent the mesh from being displaced by the direct defect.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic suction cannula to evacuate CO2 from the abdominal cavity, allowing intra-abdominal pressure to naturally expel the gas without the need for active suction.

The surgeon uses a six-inch suture to close the surgical site, leaving a small gap for the needle driver. The assistant inserts a suction cannula to allow CO₂ to escape, quickly evacuating the pocket. The surgeon checks the mesh placement, ensuring it sits properly, especially posterior laterally, before completing the suture with two final throws.
